title: The last key-cutter of Lisbon
summary: An old locksmith in Lisbon teaches his young apprentice why he still cuts keys by hand.

body.speaker: Senhor Costa
body.text: Hold the key flat, Diogo. Do not press hard. The metal will tell you when to stop.

body.speaker: Diogo
body.text: But the machine in the new shop is faster. It cuts a key in ten seconds. Mine takes five minutes.

body.speaker: Senhor Costa
body.text: Yes, the machine is faster. But the machine cannot open an old lock. The old houses here in Alfama have locks from a hundred years ago. Every lock is different.

body.speaker: Diogo
body.text: So why do people still come to you?

body.speaker: Senhor Costa
body.text: Last week a woman came. Her mother died, and she had a small wooden box. She lost the key. The box was from her grandmother. The machine could not help her. I looked at the lock for an hour. Then I cut a key by hand. The box opened.

body.speaker: Senhor Costa
body.text: Letters. Old letters, and a ring. The woman cried. She did not pay me for a key. She paid me for the letters.

body.speaker: Diogo
body.text: My father says this work is dying. He says I should learn computers.

body.speaker: Senhor Costa
body.text: Your father is not wrong. Fewer people come every year. But while one old lock is still in this city, someone must know how to open it. After me, that someone is you.

body.speaker: Diogo
body.text: And when the last old lock is gone?

body.speaker: Senhor Costa
body.text: Then you will teach a young person why we once cut keys by hand. That is not nothing. Now, hold the key flat again. Slowly. Feel the metal.

annotations.meaningVi: kim loại sẽ cho bạn biết

annotations.phrase: I cut a key by hand
annotations.meaningVi: tôi cắt chìa khóa bằng tay
điều đó không phải là vô nghĩa

grammarNotes.title: 'Fewer' and 'less' for countable and uncountable nouns
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'fewer' với danh từ đếm được (số nhiều) và 'less' với danh từ không đếm được. Trong bài: 'Fewer people come every year.' 'People' đếm được nên dùng 'fewer'. Ngược lại nói 'less water', 'less time' (không đếm được). Quy tắc: fewer + danh từ số nhiều; less + danh từ không đếm được.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses 'fewer' with countable (plural) nouns and 'less' with uncountable nouns. From the dialogue: 'Fewer people come every year.' 'People' is countable, so 'fewer' is correct. Compare 'less water', 'less time' (uncountable). Rule: fewer + plural noun; less + uncountable noun.

translationVi:
Senhor Costa: Giữ chìa khóa nằm phẳng, Diogo. Đừng ấn mạnh. Kim loại sẽ cho cháu biết khi nào dừng.

Diogo: Nhưng cái máy ở cửa hàng mới nhanh hơn. Nó cắt một chìa trong mười giây. Cháu mất năm phút.

Senhor Costa: Đúng, cái máy nhanh hơn. Nhưng cái máy không mở được ổ khóa cũ. Những ngôi nhà cổ ở Alfama đây có ổ khóa từ một trăm năm trước. Mỗi ổ khóa một khác.

Diogo: Vậy tại sao người ta vẫn đến chỗ thầy?

Senhor Costa: Tuần trước một người phụ nữ đến. Mẹ bà ấy mất, và bà có một cái hộp gỗ nhỏ. Bà làm mất chìa. Cái hộp là của bà ngoại. Cái máy không giúp được bà. Ta nhìn ổ khóa một tiếng đồng hồ. Rồi ta cắt một chìa bằng tay. Cái hộp mở ra.

Diogo: Bên trong có gì?

Senhor Costa: Những lá thư. Thư cũ, và một chiếc nhẫn. Người phụ nữ khóc. Bà không trả tiền cho ta vì một cái chìa. Bà trả tiền cho những lá thư.

Diogo: Cha cháu nói nghề này đang mai một. Cha bảo cháu nên học máy tính.

Senhor Costa: Cha cháu không sai. Mỗi năm ngày càng ít người đến. Nhưng khi còn một ổ khóa cũ trong thành phố này, phải có ai đó biết cách mở nó. Sau ta, người đó là cháu.

Diogo: Và khi ổ khóa cũ cuối cùng biến mất?

Senhor Costa: Thì cháu sẽ dạy một người trẻ vì sao xưa kia chúng ta cắt chìa bằng tay. Điều đó không phải vô nghĩa. Nào, giữ chìa nằm phẳng lại. Chậm thôi. Cảm nhận kim loại.

questions.explanation: Summary and context — he is learning the trade.

questions.prompt: How long does Diogo take to cut a key?
What can the machine NOT do, according to Senhor Costa?
questions.explanation: 'The machine cannot open an old lock.'

questions.prompt: Why did the woman come to Senhor Costa?
options: She lost the key to her grandmother's wooden box.
questions.explanation: 'She had a small wooden box. She lost the key.'

questions.prompt: How long did he look at the lock?
questions.explanation: 'I looked at the lock for an hour.'

options: That Diogo should buy a machine.
That Diogo should keep cutting keys.

questions.prompt: Does Senhor Costa think the father is wrong?
options: No — he agrees fewer people come, but says someone must still know the skill.
Yes — he says the father knows nothing.
He tells Diogo to leave at once.
questions.explanation: 'Your father is not wrong. Fewer people come every year. But...'

questions.prompt: What does Senhor Costa say Diogo will do when the last old lock is gone?
options: Teach a young person why they once cut keys by hand.
questions.explanation: 'You will teach a young person why we once cut keys by hand.'

questions.prompt: What does Costa mean by 'She paid me for the letters'?
options: The real value was the memories the key unlocked, not the key itself.
She owed money for old mail.
questions.explanation: The worth was emotional, beyond the price of cutting a key.

options: A dying craft survives not by speed but by the human meaning it can still unlock.
questions.explanation: The hand-cut key opens what no machine can — memory.

cloze.template:

Diogo: But the machine in the new shop is faster. It cuts a key in ten {{b2}}. Mine takes five minutes.
Senhor Costa: Yes, the machine is faster. But the machine cannot open an old {{b3}}. The old houses here in Alfama have locks from a hundred years ago. Every lock is different.
Diogo: So why do people still come to you?
Senhor Costa: Last week a {{b4}} came. Her mother died, and she had a small wooden {{b5}}. She lost the key. The box was from her grandmother. The machine could not help her. I looked at the lock for an {{b6}}. Then I cut a key by {{b7}}. The box opened.
Diogo: What was inside?
Senhor Costa: Letters. Old letters, and a ring. The woman {{b8}}. She did not pay me for a key. She paid me for the letters.
Diogo: My father says this work is dying. He says I should learn computers.
Senhor Costa: Your father is not wrong. {{b9}} people come every year. But while one old lock is still in this city, someone must know how to open it. After me, that someone is you.
Diogo: And when the last old lock is gone?
Senhor Costa: Then you will teach a young person why we once cut keys by hand. That is not nothing. Now, hold the key {{b10}} again. Slowly. Feel the metal.

blanks.explanation: Looked at the lock for an hour.
I cut a key by hand.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Senhor Costa says teaching the next person 'why we once cut keys by hand' is 'not nothing'. Is there a skill in your family or town that is fading? What would be lost if no one remembered how it was done?
